hardening off: Gradually getting indoor-grown seedlings used to outdoor conditions over 7–10 days. You move them outside for increasing periods so they don't get shocked by wind, sun, and cold.
pricking out: Carefully moving tiny seedlings from a seed tray into their own individual pots. You do this when they have their first 'true' leaves (not the initial seed leaves).
potting on: Moving a plant into a bigger pot because it's outgrown its current one. You'll see roots poking out the bottom — that's your cue.
bolting: When a plant suddenly shoots up a tall flower stalk and stops producing leaves. Common in lettuce, spinach, and herbs during hot weather. Usually means it's too late to harvest.
deadheading: Snipping off spent flowers to encourage the plant to produce more blooms instead of wasting energy making seeds.
pinching out: Removing the growing tip of a plant to encourage it to branch out sideways instead of growing tall and leggy. Essential for tomatoes and sweet peas.
companion planting: Growing certain plants together because they help each other — like marigolds near tomatoes to deter pests, or basil near peppers to improve flavour.
NPK: The three key nutrients plants need: Nitrogen (N) for leaves, Phosphorus (P) for roots and flowers, Potassium (K) for fruit and overall health. The numbers on fertiliser bags show the ratio.
pH: A measure of how acidic or alkaline your soil is, on a scale of 0–14. Most plants prefer slightly acidic soil (6.0–7.0). Blueberries like it acidic (4.5), lavender likes it alkaline (7.5).
germination: The moment a seed sprouts and starts growing. It's triggered by the right combination of warmth, moisture, and sometimes light.
stratification: Tricking seeds into thinking they've been through winter by chilling them in the fridge for a few weeks. Some wildflower and tree seeds need this before they'll germinate.
true leaves: The second set of leaves a seedling produces. The first pair (cotyledons) are seed leaves that come pre-packed in the seed — true leaves are the 'real' ones.
cotyledons: The first leaves to emerge from a seed. They look different from the plant's normal leaves and contain stored food to get the seedling started.
damping off: A fungal disease that kills seedlings — they flop over at soil level. Caused by too much moisture and poor air circulation. Keep things airy!
mulching: Covering the soil surface with organic material (bark, compost, straw) to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate temperature.
top dressing: Adding a thin layer of compost or fertiliser to the soil surface around a plant without digging it in. Good for container plants.
rootbound: When a plant's roots have completely filled its pot and start circling. The plant needs repotting or the roots need teasing apart.
leggy: When seedlings grow tall and thin with long stems, usually because they're not getting enough light. They stretch towards the nearest light source.
transplant shock: Temporary wilting or stunted growth after moving a plant to a new location. Give it water, shade, and time to recover.
cold frame: A bottomless box with a clear lid (often an old window) used to protect plants from cold weather. Like a mini greenhouse.
polytunnel: A large tunnel covered in polythene plastic, used to extend the growing season. Cheaper than a greenhouse.
cloche: A small clear cover placed over individual plants to protect them from cold or pests. Like a mini glass dome.
fleece: Lightweight fabric draped over plants to protect from frost. Lets light and rain through but traps warmth. A gardener's best friend in spring.
successional sowing: Sowing small batches of seeds every 2–3 weeks so you have a continuous harvest rather than everything ready at once.
thinning: Removing excess seedlings so the remaining ones have room to grow properly. Hard to do emotionally, essential for healthy plants.
earthing up: Mounding soil around the base of a plant. Done with potatoes to encourage more tubers, and with leeks to blanch the stems white.
blanching: Blocking light from part of a plant to keep it pale and tender. Done with leeks (earthing up) and celery (wrapping in cardboard).
crown: The point where the stem meets the roots, usually at soil level. Many plants rot if the crown is buried too deep.
runner: A horizontal stem that grows along the ground and produces new plants at its nodes. Strawberries spread this way.
sucker: A shoot that grows from the rootstock or base of a plant. On tomatoes, these are side shoots that should be removed ('pinched out').
side shoot: A stem that grows from the joint between the main stem and a leaf. Tomato side shoots should be removed to direct energy to fruit.
determinate: A tomato variety that grows to a set height and produces all its fruit at once. Good for containers and small spaces.
indeterminate: A tomato variety that keeps growing and fruiting until frost kills it. Needs staking and regular pinching out of side shoots.
cordon: A single-stemmed fruit tree or tomato trained vertically. Side shoots are removed to keep one main stem.
F1 hybrid: A first-generation cross between two parent varieties, bred for specific traits like disease resistance or higher yield. Seeds from F1 plants won't come true.
come true: When seeds produce plants identical to the parent. Open-pollinated and heirloom varieties come true; F1 hybrids don't.
heirloom: An old, open-pollinated plant variety that's been passed down for generations. Often has better flavour but less disease resistance than modern hybrids.
perennial: A plant that lives for more than two years. It dies back in winter and regrows in spring. Rhubarb, asparagus, and most herbs are perennials.
annual: A plant that completes its entire lifecycle (seed → flower → seed → death) in one growing season. Tomatoes, basil, and sunflowers are annuals.
biennial: A plant that takes two years to complete its lifecycle. It grows leaves in year one, then flowers and sets seed in year two. Foxglove and parsley are biennials.
hardy: A plant that can survive frost and cold winters outdoors without protection. In the UK, fully hardy means it'll survive down to -15°C.
half-hardy: A plant that can handle light frost but needs protection from hard frost. Typical example: sweet peas.
tender: A plant that can't handle any frost. Tomatoes, peppers, and basil are tender — one cold night can kill them.
growing season: The period between the last frost in spring and the first frost in autumn. In Bristol, roughly April to October.
last frost date: The average date of the last frost in spring for your area. In Bristol, typically mid-April. Key for planning when to plant out tender crops.
sowing window: The ideal date range for planting seeds. Sow too early and seedlings get leggy waiting for warmth; too late and they won't mature before winter.
loam: The ideal soil type — a balanced mix of sand, silt, and clay. Holds moisture but drains well. Most plants love it.
ericaceous: Acidic compost (pH 4–5) designed for acid-loving plants like blueberries, azaleas, and heathers. Don't use it for vegetables.
perlite: White, lightweight volcanic granules mixed into compost to improve drainage and aeration. Looks like tiny polystyrene balls.
vermiculite: A mineral that expands when heated, used in seed compost to hold moisture. Good for seed starting.
liquid feed: Fertiliser dissolved in water and applied when watering. Tomato feed (high potash) is the most common type in UK gardens.
potash: Another name for potassium (K). High-potash feeds encourage flowering and fruiting. Tomato feed is high in potash.
nitrogen: The 'N' in NPK. Promotes leafy green growth. Too much makes plants lush but delays flowering and fruiting.
